pr.title: Label stage pointers and fixup some of the BSS
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: PR 110 was an assembly/data cleanup that labeled many raw stage-related pointer constants and refined BSS/sdata boundaries so those pointers could target symbols instead of hard-coded addresses. The main mechanical change was replacing `.4byte 0x80...` entries in stage descriptor/table data with `.4byte lbl_...` references, while splitting large `.skip` blocks in `asm/bss.s` and adding missing `.global` labels in `asm/data.s` and `asm/sdata.s` at the corresponding addresses.

key_files.role: Largest change; converted many raw stage table pointer words to symbolic `lbl_...` references and introduced additional labels inside stage-related data blocks.

Split oversized BSS `.skip` ranges into smaller labeled regions such as `lbl_80470708`, `lbl_80470850`, `lbl_8047086C`, `lbl_80472AF8`, `lbl_80480530`, and `lbl_804831A8` so data tables could reference them symbolically.
Added missing small-data labels around embedded strings/constants such as `lbl_804D47B8`, `lbl_804D4824`, `lbl_804D4838`, `lbl_804D4968`, `lbl_804D4998`, `lbl_804D49A0`, and `lbl_804D4CD8`.

what_changed: Replaced many hard-coded pointer constants in `asm/data.s` with labels, for example `0x804831A8` became `lbl_804831A8`, `0x804807B0` became `lbl_804807B0`, and repeated `0x80472AF8`/`0x8047086C` pairs became `lbl_80472AF8`/`lbl_8047086C`.

Split broad BSS reservation blocks into explicit labeled slices without changing the apparent address layout; examples include splitting `lbl_804706D8` into `lbl_804706D8`, `lbl_80470708`, `lbl_80470850`, `lbl_8047086C`, and `lbl_80472AF8`.

Added BSS symbols for stage pointer targets in the `8047xxxx`, `8048xxxx`, `8049xxxx`, and `804D68xx`/`804D69xx` ranges, including `lbl_8047E2A4`, `lbl_80480530`, `lbl_80480668`, `lbl_804807B0`, `lbl_804950F0`, `lbl_80495370`, `lbl_804D6860`, `lbl_804D68D0`, and `lbl_804D6948`.

Added data-section labels around stage descriptor substructures in the `803E48xx` and later ranges, then changed nearby self-references from raw addresses like `0x803E48F4`, `0x803E4944`, and `0x803E48C8` to symbolic labels.
Added small-data labels in `asm/sdata.s` at boundaries before strings and constants, including labels near `"/GrVe"`, float-looking words such as `0x41800000`, and other stage/resource-related data.
smart_moves: The PR did not merely rename constants in tables; it first established real labels at the BSS addresses those constants pointed to, making the symbolic references assemblable and preserving layout.
Large `.skip` reservations were split at exact pointer-target offsets, which is a low-risk way to improve symbol coverage while keeping BSS size and ordering intact.
Repeated pointer pairs were converted consistently across many stage table entries, making patterns easier to search and reducing the chance of later treating the same address as unrelated data.
The data-table labels were added at internal structure boundaries before replacing self-referential raw addresses, which improves readability without requiring full type recovery yet.
decomp_lessons: When decompiling data-heavy systems, replace raw `.4byte 0x80...` addresses only after adding labels at the target locations; otherwise the diff can hide whether the target is actually aligned and section-correct.
BSS fixups often mean subdividing existing `.skip` blocks, not changing total storage. Preserve aggregate sizes and addresses while exposing the offsets that code/data already reference.

Repeated stage table entries are useful evidence for discovering shared buffers or per-stage resource structures; label the repeated targets early so later C structs can use named externs instead of magic addresses.
Adding labels in `sdata` around strings, floats, and small constants can unblock later pointer cleanup in `data.s`, even if the semantic names are not yet known.
A symbolization PR can be valuable even without semantic names: address-stable `lbl_...` symbols make future struct extraction and relocation reasoning easier.
naming_conventions: Used the existing anonymous-label convention `lbl_XXXXXXXX` with uppercase hexadecimal addresses for newly exposed data/BSS/sdata symbols.
Kept function references as existing `func_XXXXXXXX` or `lbl_XXXXXXXX` symbols rather than inventing semantic function names.
Used `.global lbl_...` immediately before each newly exposed assembly label.
Preserved existing `NULL` usage for zero pointer entries instead of changing them to numeric zeroes.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Convert `.4byte 0x804xxxxx` and `.4byte 0x803xxxxx` pointer constants to `.4byte lbl_...` once a matching label exists in the correct section.
Split `.skip` directives at exact address boundaries to create labels while keeping the total reserved byte count equivalent.

Label internal data-table boundaries where nearby entries contain self-pointers or table pointers, as shown by new labels such as `lbl_803E48F4` and `lbl_803E4944` followed by replacements of raw `0x803E48F4`/`0x803E4944` references.

Use repeated raw address patterns as a guide for BSS subdivision; examples in this PR include repeated references to `lbl_80472AF8`, `lbl_8047086C`, `lbl_80480530`, `lbl_80479D98`, `lbl_804D6860`, `lbl_804D68D0`, `lbl_804D6940`, and `lbl_804D6948`.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or review records were present in the provided PR slice, so there is no direct reviewer feedback to extract.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that give semantic names or C structs to the stage tables around `lbl_803DD9A0`, `lbl_803DDC58`, `lbl_803DE1B8`, and `lbl_803DE930`.
Search for later changes involving `lbl_80472AF8` and `lbl_8047086C` to see whether these BSS buffers were identified.
Search for later changes involving `lbl_80480530`, `lbl_80480668`, `lbl_804807B0`, and `lbl_804831A8` to track stage/BSS type recovery.
Search for `lbl_803E48F4`, `lbl_803E4944`, and adjacent `803E` labels to identify the stage descriptor table family introduced here.
Check whether `asm/sdata.s` labels such as `lbl_804D47B8`, `lbl_804D4824`, and `lbl_804D4968` later received semantic names.
